title: Exploring the impact of tinnitus on work productivity
abstract:
Background/Objectives: Tinnitus affects various aspects of health, yet its impact on occupational functioning remains less well explored. This study investigated the impact of tinnitus on work productivity, the role of comorbidities of anxiety, depression, and quality of life, and explored whether an evidence-based intervention may potentially influence workplace functioning.

Methods: The study used cross-sectional and longitudinal data and employed mixed methods design to enable comparing work productivity before and after undertaking ICBT for tinnitus. Data were collected from 449 participants (64% employed; mean age of 54.4 years) who were seeking to enroll in an internet-based psychological intervention. Of these, 200 also completed an internet-based cognitive behavioral therapy intervention (ICBT). Data were analyzed using descriptive statistics, analysis of variance, qualitative content analysis and comparisons made between those whose work was unaffected due to tinnitus and those who worked less.

Results: Pre-intervention, 81% reported no change in work patterns. Because of tinnitus 11% worked fewer hours, 7% had stopped working, and 1% received disability allowance. Participants were significantly less effective in their work capacity prior to undertaking the intervention compared to post-intervention and at follow-up; for the overall sample and post-intervention improvements were observed in tinnitus distress, anxiety, depression, and insomnia. There was significant time difference between group interactions for tinnitus, anxiety and insomnia but not for depression and quality of life when comparing those working and not working. Qualitative findings highlighted challenges related to attention, fatigue, and communication, as well as coping strategies and role modifications.

Conclusions: Further studies are required to build on this exploratory research. The present findings underscore the need for workplace policies recognizing tinnitus as a potential disability and providing reasonable accommodation and options to access tinnitus interventions. Clinical interventions should also consider how to improve workplace functioning in individuals with bothersome tinnitus. Future research should explore ways to address cognitive load and enhance productivity.

Abstract 
19
 
Background/Objectives: Tinnitus affects various aspects of health, yet its impact on occupational 
20
 
functioning remains less well explored. This study investigated the impact of tinnitus on work 
21
 
productivity, the role of comorbidities of anxiety, depression, and quality of life, and to explore 
22
 
whether an evidence-based intervention may potentially influence workplace functioning. Meth-
23
 
ods: The study used cross-sectional and longitudinal data and employed mixed methods design to 
24
 
enable comparing work productivity before and after undertaking ICBT for tinnitus. Data were col-
25
 
lected from 449 participants (64% employed; mean age of 54.4 years) who were seeking to enroll in 
26
 
an internet-based psychological intervention. Of these, 200 also completed an Internet-based cogni-
27
 
tive behavioral therapy intervention (ICBT). Data were analyzed using descriptive statistics, analy-
28
 
sis of variance, and qualitative content analysis and comparisons made between those whose work 
29
 
was unaffected due to tinnitus and those who worked less. Results: Pre-intervention, 81% reported 
30
 
no change in work patterns. Because of tinnitus 11% worked fewer hours, 7% had stopped working, 
31
 
and 1% received disability allowance. Participants were significantly less effective in their work ca-
32
 
pacity prior to undertaking the intervention compared to post intervention and at follow-up, for the 
33
 
overall sample and post-intervention improvements were observed in tinnitus distress, anxiety, de-
34
 
pression, and insomnia. There were significant time by group interactions for tinnitus, anxiety and 
35
 
insomnia but not for depression and quality of life when comparing those working and not work-
36
 
ing. Qualitative findings highlighted challenges related to attention, fatigue, and communication, 
37
 
as well as coping strategies and role modifications. Conclusions: Further studies are required to 
38
 
build on this exploratory research. The present findings underscore the need for workplace policies 
39
 
recognizing tinnitus as a potential disability and providing reasonable accommodation and options 
40
 
to access tinnitus interventions. Clinical interventions should also consider how to improve work-
41
 
place functioning in individuals with bothersome tinnitus. Future research should explore ways to 
42
 
address cognitive load and enhance productivity. 
43
 
Keywords: Tinnitus; Workplace functioning; Productivity; Cognitive load; Intervention; Occupa-
44
 
tional health

Brain Sci. 2023, 13, x FOR PEER REVIEW

1. Introduction 
47
 
Strong economies rely on high productivity and employment rates. Factors such as 
48
 
absenteeism and reduced work performance can negatively impact economic growth [1]. 
49
 
Identifying strategies to enhance productivity is therefore essential and requires multifac-
50
 
eted approaches. This may involve protocols and policies to accommodate health-related 
51
 
difficulties. One potentially overlooked factor is the contribution of tinnitus to workplace 
52
 
functioning and productivity. 
53
 
Tinnitus is defined as the conscious perception of sound in the absence of an external 
54
 
auditory stimulus. Its prevalence is estimated at 15%, although only a subset of individu-
55
 
als experiences bothersome tinnitus that interferes with daily functioning [2].  The pri-
56
 
mary risk factors for tinnitus include hearing loss and noise exposure, with occupations 
57
 
such as the military, construction and manufacturing, involving high noise levels, typi-
58
 
cally above 85 dBA [3]. Despite this association, the impact of tinnitus on work ability has 
59
 
received limited attention.  
60
 
Tinnitus symptoms may be exacerbated in quiet environments, during stressful or 
61
 
emotionally demanding situations, when tasks require sustained concentration, and dur-
62
 
ing social interactions or sudden changes in sound environments [4]. These conditions are 
63
 
common in workplace settings, suggesting that tinnitus could significantly affect occupa-
64
 
tional functioning and may lead to earlier retirement [5]. Having tinnitus and hyperacusis 
65
 
predicted reduced work capacity and that hyperacusis predicted work absence [6]. Fur-
66
 
thermore, hearing and tinnitus-related difficulties were predictors of long-term work in-
67
 
capacity [7]. Certain noisy occupations are associated with an increased risk of bother-
68
 
some tinnitus, and hence more likely to lead to a reduction in work capacity [8] particu-
69
 
larly for military veterans [9]. It has furthermore been found that for those on a disability 
70
 
pension for hearing loss, vertigo or tinnitus, there is a 14% increased risk of mortality [10], 
71
 
making finding ways to reduce the impact of these disabilities important. There are thus 
72
 
indications that tinnitus can affect work productivity. What is not clear is if there are any 
73
 
interventions that can help minimize the impact of tinnitus. There were for instance no 
74
 
identified studies that have examined the effect of tinnitus interventions on workplace 
75
 
productivity.    
76
 
Cognitive mechanisms may further explain reduced productivity. Tinnitus imposes 
77
 
an additional cognitive load, competing for limited working memory resources, particu-
78
 
larly under demanding conditions [11]. Possible mechanisms include the associated with 
79
 
hyperactivity in the fronto-parietal attention networks resulting in delayed attentional dis-
80
 
engagement and poorer updating of working memory buffers [12]. Consequently, indi-
81
 
viduals with tinnitus demonstrate slower reaction times and poorer performance on at-
82
 
tention-switching tasks compared to those without tinnitus [13]. Comorbidities such as 
83
 
hearing loss, insomnia and anxiety may compound these effects, further impairing per-
84
 
formance on tasks requiring high attentional control and reducing cognitive capacity [14]. 
85
 
Although these links have been made, there is still a lack of clarity regarding the impact 
86
 
tinnitus has on functioning at work and whether functioning can be improved.  
87
 
Although evidence-based interventions such as cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) 
88
 
have been shown to reduce tinnitus distress and associated comorbidities [15], little is 
89
 
known about whether such interventions improve workplace functioning. To address this 
90
 
gap, the present study aimed to quantify the impact of tinnitus on work productivity and 
91
 
examine whether these effects can be mitigated through intervention. Specifically, the fol-
92
 
lowing research questions were explored: (i) What impact does tinnitus have on the ability 
93
 
to work? (ii) Do comorbidities such as hearing loss and depression further reduce work 
94
 
productivity? and (iii) Can an evidence-based tinnitus intervention improve workplace 
95
 
functioning? 
96
 
2. Materials and Methods 
97
 
2.1. Study Design 
98
 
Brain Sci. 2023, 13, x FOR PEER REVIEW 
3 of 18 
 
The study used a mixed methods design and was nested in clinical trials (Clinical 
99
 
Trials.gov registration numbers NCT04004260, NCT04335812) that were aimed at evalu-
100
 
ating the efficacy of internet-based cognitive behavioral therapy (ICBT) for tinnitus 
101
 
[15,16]. Ethics approval was granted by the Institutional Review Board at Lamar Univer-
102
 
sity (IRB-FY17-209, approved on 07.06.2019 and IRB-FY20-200 approved on 02.04.2000). 
103
 
All participants completed informed consent prior to participating in the study. 
104
 
2.2. Participants 
105
 
Both cross-sectional and longitudinal data (pre-intervention, post-intervention and 
106
 
at 2-month follow up) were used based on the pooled data from the studies. Change in 
107
 
workplace productivity after intervention was assessed but has not been reported before. 
108
 
All participants (n=449) who were enrolled in the ICBT clinical trials were asked to com-
109
 
plete series of questionnaires before and after the intervention through the online platform 
110
 
(i.e., iTerapi) that was used to administer ICBT. The inclusion (i.e., adults >18 years, access 
111
 
to computer, having tinnitus for longer than 3 months, at least a mild severity of tinnitus) 
112
 
and exclusion criteria (i.e., having significant depression, psychiatric condition, pulsatile 
113
 
or objective tinnitus, currently undergoing other tinnitus therapies) are detailed in the 
114
 
clinical trial publications [16,17,18]. Chronic tinnitus was defined as experiencing tinnitus 
115
 
for at least 3 months or longer. 
116
 
2.3. Data Collection 
117
 
Data collection took place at three time points, pre-intervention, post-intervention 
118
 
and two months follow-up. The baseline questionnaire included detailed demographic 
119
 
and hearing-related questions. In addition, various structured and unstructured question-
120
 
naires were administered at all three time points as detailed in the clinical trials reporting 
121
 
[16,17,18]. 
122
 
The following work-related questions and some general measures were used in this 
123
 
study.  
124
 
i. 
Do you work less due to having tinnitus? (no or yes. If yes, select reduced 
125
 
hours, stopped working, disability allowance?). 
126
 
ii. 
Has the tinnitus experience made you less effective at doing your work or 
127
 
daily tasks? (No, slightly, considerable, very much so). 
128
 
iii. 
The Tinnitus Functional Index (TFI) [19]; to measure the degree of tinnitus 
129
 
distress. 
130
 
iv. 
To assess common comorbidities associated with tinnitus the following ques-
131
 
tionnaires were selected. To assess anxiety, the Generalized Anxiety Disor-
132
 
der Questionnaire (GAD7) [20]. To assess depression, the Patient Health 
133
 
Questionnaire 9; (PHQ9) [21]. The insomnia-severity index (ISI) [22]; and 
134
 
health-related quality of life (Euroqol EQ-5D-5L), [23] were also included. 
135
 
v. 
Open-ended question: What describes your work (select from Entry level or 
136
 
unskilled work, skilled/ professional work, skilled or professional work, re-
137
 
tired, not working) 
138
 
From question (i) the study sample was categorized into two subgroups based on 
139
 
whether their work was unaffected by tinnitus (answering no) or affected resulting in 
140
 
working reduced hours (answered yes) to compare the response. 
141
 
2.4 ICBT Intervention 
142
 
The ICBT intervention was designed to combine psychoeducation, relaxation training, 
143
 
cognitive behavioural therapy strategies, behavioral activation to enable long-term self 
144
 
management of tinnitus [24]. To improve accessibility it was delivered on a secure web 
145
 
platform, for which participants used a password protected login. The intervention was 
146
 
undertaken over an 8 week period. During this time 2-3 modules were shared per week 
147
 
as outlined in Table 1. Modules contained a mixture of information, videos, quizzes, 
148
 
Brain Sci. 2023, 13, x FOR PEER REVIEW 
4 of 18 
 
diagrams, suggested techniques to apply to daily life, worksheets to keep track of 
149
 
progress, solutions for common problems, and downloadable information. Audiological 
150
 
Guidance was provided by an experienced Tinnitus Clinician (EB) together with 
151
 
supervision from a licenced Psychologist (GA). Guidance aimed to support individuals 
152
 
who participated in the intervention via a two-way messaging system within the e-
153
 
Platform. Guidance included monitoring progress, monitoring weekly scores, providing 
154
 
weekly feedback on worksheets completed, outlining the content of new modules, and 
155
 
answering questions.  
156

158
 
2.5. Data Analysis 
159
 
Quantitative statistical analysis was done using Jamovi (version 2.3.28.0). Descriptive 
160
 
statistics included age, gender, ethnicity, race, and tinnitus characteristics. Chi-square 
161
 
analysis and t-tests were used to compare group differences. Clinical variables were tin-
162
 
nitus distress, anxiety, depression and quality of life. Repeated measures ANOVAs were 
163
 
run and Bonferroni corrected post-hoc testing between the groups when needed. Where 
164
 
Brain Sci. 2023, 13, x FOR PEER REVIEW 
5 of 18 
 
the data did not fit parametric assumptions, non-parametric testing was performed. Re-
165
 
sponse to open-ended questions were coded using qualitative content analysis [25] using 
166
 
NVivo software. 
167
 
3. Results 
168
 
3.1. Clinical Characteristics 
169
 
There were 449 participants completing the questionnaire, although some questions were not an-
170
 
swered by all participants. Of these the majority were not working less (n = 285, 63%). Of those not 
171
 
working or working reduced hours (n = 164, 27%) 126 (77%) had retired and 38 (23%) stopped work-
172
 
ing prior to retirement. There were significant gender differences as there were more females who 
173
 
were not working than males. There were also significantly more Hispanic participants working but 
174
 
no differences in Race representation. There were no group differences regarding tinnitus duration 
175
 
as shown in Table 2.

3.3. Clinical Scores 
203
 
For all the clinical scores there were significant improvements at post-intervention 
204
 
and follow-up compared to pre-intervention as seen in Figure 2. There were significant 
205
 
group differences for tinnitus distress and depression but not for anxiety and quality of 
206
 
life. There was significant time by group interactions for tinnitus, anxiety and insomnia 
207
 
but not for depression and quality of life as shown in Table 6. 
208

3.4. Qualitative analysis 
220
 
There were 310 participants who provided responses to the open questions. re-
221
 
sponses. Of these responses, 67 (22%) explained that they have not allowed the tinnitus to 
222
 
interfere with their work and 20 (6%) have found strategies to overcome the tinnitus. The 
223
 
remaining 223 (72%) comments indicated how tinnitus impacted their effectiveness work-
224
 
ing as seen in Table 5. The main themes indicating negative effects were disrupted produc-
225
 
tivity, tinnitus impacting social interactions, reduced work fulfilment, and role changes. 
226
 
A further theme was identified, indicating adaptation strategies to mitigate the effects of 
227
 
tinnitus at work. This also indicated both direct and indirect impacts. Direct impacts in-
228
 
cluded disrupted productivity due to difficulty sustaining attention, slower task comple-
229
 
tion, not hearing critical information, not meeting the demands and increased errors. In-
230
 
direct effects encompassed fatigue, withdrawing, higher anxiety and depression, in-
231
 
creased frustration and lower work enjoyment as seen in Table 7. 
232

4. Discussion 
234
 
Tinnitus imposes significant healthcare and societal costs [26], highlighting the need 
235
 
for strategies to mitigate its impact. This study examined the effects of tinnitus on work-
236
 
place functioning, the role of comorbidities, and whether an evidence-based intervention 
237
 
could improve occupational outcomes. During this preliminary study, participants re-
238
 
ported higher work productivity at post-intervention and follow-up compared to pre-in-
239
 
tervention. Post-intervention improvements were observed in tinnitus distress, anxiety, 
240
 
depression, and insomnia. There was significant time by group interactions for tinnitus 
241
 
and anxiety when comparing those working and not working. Qualitative findings high-
242
 
lighted challenges related to attention, fatigue, and communication, as well as coping 
243
 
Brain Sci. 2023, 13, x FOR PEER REVIEW 
14 of 18 
 
strategies and role modifications. This study contributes to the tinnitus intervention liter-
244
 
ature by indicating that undertaking an evidence-based intervention may improve work 
245
 
productivity as outlined in the sections below. This relationship should be further as-
246
 
sessed in more robust study designs including a control group.   
247
 
4.1. Impact of Tinnitus on Workplace Functioning 
248
 
  
Most participants (81%) reported no change in work patterns due to tinnitus. These 
249
 
results are similar to those found by Coco et al [8]. reporting that 83% of veterans did not 
250
 
report an impact of tinnitus on work. According to the current research, the remaining 11% 251
 
worked less, 7% stopped working, and 1% received disability allowance. Although global 
252
 
figures for tinnitus-related disability benefits are unavailable, these rates are substantially 
253
 
lower than those observed in the veteran population in the US, where approximately 3.2 
254
 
million veterans receive disability compensation for tinnitus and 273 502 new claims are 
255
 
approved annually [27]. This discrepancy may reflect established reporting channels 
256
 
within military systems. In contrast, many individuals in civilian settings may avoid 
257
 
disclosing tinnitus-related difficulties due to fear of stigma or discrimination, potentially 
258
 
leading to underreporting and missed career advancement opportunities [28]. The use of 
259
 
items used for workplace productivity and functioning were unvalidated and relied on 
260
 
subjective self-reported perception that could have introduced bias recall. Incorporating 
261
 
validated measures for future studies is required. 
262
 
In addition to working less because of tinnitus as highlighted above, tinnitus also had 
263
 
impact on work productivity. Due to tinnitus 11% were working reduced hours, 7% 
264
 
stopped working and 1% were on disability allowance. The present study indicates that 
265
 
more awareness regarding the impact of tinnitus in workplaces is needed, to accommo-
266
 
date the challenges faced by these employees and enable them to continue working 
267
 
4.2. Comorbidities and Reduced Productivity 
268
 
Qualitative analysis revealed that 22% of participants actively prevented tinnitus 
269
 
from interfering with work, with 6% employing coping strategies. Among those reporting 
270
 
work-related difficulties, tinnitus disrupted productivity by impairing sustained atten-
271
 
tion, increasing fatigue, complicating work in quiet environments, and causing slower 
272
 
task completion and more errors. Communication challenges were noted, including 
273
 
missed information and social withdrawal. Reduced work fulfillment was linked to 
274
 
heightened anxiety, depression, frustration, and irritability, often resulting in role modi-
275
 
fications or career changes. Both direct impacts and indirect impacts were found such as 
276
 
disrupted productivity due to difficulty sustaining attention, slower task completion, not 
277
 
hearing critical information, not meeting the demands and increased errors. Indirect ef-
278
 
fects encompassed fatigue, withdrawing, higher anxiety and depression, increased frus-
279
 
tration and lower work enjoyment. These findings align with previous research indicating 
280
 
that tinnitus-related comorbidities, particularly anxiety and depression, exacerbate occu-
281
 
pational challenges [29]. Anxiety and depression are furthermore likely to be the greatest 
282
 
predictors of the impact on daily life from prediction modeling including 21 tinnitus stud-
283
 
ies [30]. The combination of fatigue, cognitive demands, increased anxiety and depression 
284
 
may all be contributing to reduced productivity.   
285
 
4.3. Effects of Evidence-Based Intervention on Work Productivity 
286
 
This study was unique in its approach comparing work productivity before and after 
287
 
undertaking ICBT for tinnitus. The results are considered an indication of possible effects, 
288
 
as only half of the overall sample completed the intervention and post-intervention ques-
289
 
tionnaires and fewer the 2 month follow-up questionnaire and there was no control group 
290
 
Brain Sci. 2023, 13, x FOR PEER REVIEW 
15 of 18 
 
for this study. The preliminary findings indicate that following the intervention, signifi-
291
 
cantly fewer participants reported working reduced hours at both post-intervention and 
292
 
follow-up compared to pre-intervention levels. This improvement was observed in the 
293
 
overall sample and among those who were employed. In addition, tinnitus-related dis-
294
 
tress and comorbidities, including anxiety, depression, and quality of life, showed signif-
295
 
icant improvements at post-intervention and follow-up relative to baseline scores. More-
296
 
over, qualitative feedback supported these findings, with participants indicating that the 
297
 
intervention enhanced their ability to work. Reduced reports of working less after the in-
298
 
tervention suggest that targeted tinnitus management may mitigate occupational limita-
299
 
tions. These results may not be fully directly associated with undertaking the intervention 
300
 
as they did not account for various possible confounding variables such as whether par-
301
 
ticipants received concurrent tinnitus-related treatments during the study period or 
302
 
whether or not there were changes to their work environment or other life stressors. Fur-
303
 
ther studies need to adjust for these possible confounding variables.  
304
 
4.4. Policy Implications 
305
 
Existing policies and legal frameworks address hearing loss through noise exposure 
306
 
regulations and disability legislation. Within these frameworks, tinnitus should be explic-
307
 
itly recognized as a potential disability. Standardized workplace policies are needed to 
308
 
ensure employees with tinnitus can access reasonable adjustments, such as strategies to 
309
 
improve concentration (e.g., access to tinnitus maskers, or specialized headphones of 
310
 
headsets) and reduce cognitive load. Such adjustments may include flexible scheduling, 
311
 
social support, access to healthcare, optimized workspaces, and assistive listening devices 
312
 
to improve overall functionality. A large proportion of individuals with tinnitus also have 
313
 
hearing loss, and may benefit from the use of hearing aids in the workplace. Ways to im-
314
 
prove the availability of tinnitus interventions should furthermore be sought by employ-
315
 
ees. Employer education is furthermore critical to reduce stigma and foster supportive 
316
 
environments [31]. 
317
 
4.5. Study Limitations and Future Directions 
318
 
While the study highlighted impact of tinnitus on work functioning and more im-
319
 
portantly the effect of intervention, the study had a few limitations. The lack of a control 
320
 
or comparison group makes it difficult to determine whether the observed improvements 
321
 
are attributable to the specific effects of ICBT, natural change over time, placebo effects, or 
322
 
regression to the mean. The study relied on self-reported perception that could introduce 
323
 
recall bias and desirability bias. Furthermore, many questions such as ‘Do you work less 
324
 
due to having tinnitus’ lacked established validity and reliability evidence.  
325
 
There were potential sampling bias, due to the fact that there was a large number of 
326
 
people who failed to fill in the post intervention questionnaires, and it could be that only 
327
 
those who experienced positive changes were inclined to fill them in. There were numer-
328
 
ous reasons provided for the attrition identified, such as time-constraint, participants hav-
329
 
ing competing life priorities such as caregiving responsibilities, or disengaging either after 
330
 
receiving enough benefit or finding the questionnaire too onerous. There may be other 
331
 
potential confounding variables that may have affected the results that were not ac-
332
 
counted for such as whether participants received concurrent tinnitus-related treatments 
333
 
during the study period, changes in the work environment or change in work environ-
334
 
ments or demands. Moreover, live events and/or stressors may furthermore affect work 
335
 
productivity independently of the intervention. Future studies need to address the con-
336
 
founding variables and currently study limitations. 
337
 
 
338
 
Addressing the cognitive load associated with tinnitus is essential for improving 
339
 
work productivity. The Job Demands–Resources Model [32] offers a useful framework for 
340
 
examining how job demands, such as workload, time pressure, emotional strain, and role 
341
 
Brain Sci. 2023, 13, x FOR PEER REVIEW 
16 of 18 
 
ambiguity, interact with resources to influence outcomes. According to the Job Demands–
342
 
Resources Model, sufficient resources can buffer the negative impact of high demands, 
343
 
enhancing engagement and productivity, whereas low resources combined with high de-
344
 
mands increase the risk of burnout and absenteeism. This model could be incorporated 
345
 
during future research. More comorbidities can be included, such as cognitive difficulties, 
346
 
hearing loss and hyperacusis and various combinations of these. The focus on the impact 
347
 
of tinnitus on different types of occupational needs could also be explored to provide job 
348
 
role modifications. Research can also explore workplace interventions that reduce cogni-
349
 
tive and emotional strain, including evidence-based approaches such as CBT, sound ther-
350
 
apy, and hearing aids [32]. Implementing standardized reporting systems, workplace pol-
351
 
icies, and procedures could help identify and mitigate these effects. Digital health tools 
352
 
and integrated audiological and occupational health protocols, could furthermore be ad-
353
 
vocated to improve addressing the difficulties those with tinnitus may face in the work-
354
 
place. 
355
 
5. Conclusions 
356
 
This study illustrates possible ways tinnitus can adversely affect work performance 
357
 
and productivity, both directly and indirectly. Undertaking an evidence-based interven-
358
 
tion for tinnitus can mitigate these effects and also reduce tinnitus distress and the associ-
359
 
ated anxiety, depression and insomnia. Supporting employees in accessing tinnitus inter-
360
 
ventions may be particularly beneficial, given the significant improvements observed in 
361
 
this study.
